trajectory.description: Observation: Starting fresh with iteration 0, 0.5h time budget, 30 iterations. Need to build a working DKT pipeline and validate it quickly. Analysis: Deep Knowledge Tracing (DKT) with LSTM is a strong, fast-to-train baseline for ASSISTments. Decision: Write model.py, train.py, predict.py; train for only 2 epochs to validate pipeline and measure iteration time, then submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 1 completed successfully with a basic DKT LSTM. Test AUC-ROC is 0.7131 after only 2 epochs. Training loss dropped sharply from 0.65 to 0.598, indicating significant underfitting and clear room for improvement. The full iteration took only ~45 seconds, leaving ample time (~28 minutes) and iterations. Analysis: We need to scale up model capacity and training epochs. Increasing hidden dimensions, adding a second LSTM layer, and training longer with early stopping should raise AUC substantially. Decision: Update train.py to add ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler and early stopping. Run training with larger architecture (hidden_dim=256, embed_dim=256, num_layers=2, dropout=0.3) for up to 50 epochs with patience=5. Keep predict.py and model.py unchanged.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 2 failed due to TypeError: ReduceLROnPlateau received unexpected keyword 'verbose' in this PyTorch version. The training script crashed immediately, so no new eval was produced. We still hold the iteration 1 checkpoint with AUC=0.7131. Analysis: The fix is trivial—remove the verbose=True argument. After that, we can safely train the larger 2-layer LSTM (hidden=256, embed=256, dropout=0.3) with early stopping. Based on iteration 1 timing (~22 s/epoch for a smaller model), 30 epochs with this larger model should finish well within the remaining ~28 min budget, and early stopping may cut it even shorter. Decision: Overwrite train.py with the corrected scheduler call, then launch training for up to 30 epochs with the enlarged architecture and patience=5. Immediately submit eval on the resulting best_model.pt.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 achieved AUC-ROC 0.7579 with a 2-layer LSTM (hidden=256, embed=256, dropout=0.3) and early stopping at epoch 17. Training loss was still slowly decreasing while val AUC plateaued, suggesting we can benefit from more model capacity and a stronger prediction head. Analysis: We have ~28 minutes and 27 iterations remaining, with each training run taking ~20-40 seconds. We can safely experiment with larger hidden dimensions, a small MLP head, LayerNorm, and AdamW + cosine annealing to push AUC higher. Decision: Update model.py to use hidden=512, embed=256, add LayerNorm + MLP head. Update train.py to use AdamW and CosineAnnealingLR. Train for up to 40 epochs with patience=10 and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 4 AUC=0.7556, slightly below the best 0.7579 from iter 3. The LSTM architecture seems plateaued around 0.758 despite capacity and regularization tweaks. Analysis: Transformer-based sequence models often outperform LSTM on knowledge tracing by capturing long-range dependencies. I will implement a causal Transformer encoder that separately embeds skills and correctness, adds positional encoding, and conditions predictions on the target skill embedding. Keeping the class name DKT preserves compatibility with train.py and predict.py. Decision: Rewrite model.py with the transformer, update train.py/predict.py accordingly, and do a fast 10-epoch validation run to test the new architecture before scaling up.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 5 (Transformer) achieved AUC=0.7502, which is below the best AUC=0.7579 from iteration 3 (2-layer LSTM, hidden=256, embed=256, dropout=0.3). The Transformer underperformed in this budget. Analysis: We need to revert to the LSTM architecture that produced the best result so far. To squeeze more performance, I'll add LayerNorm after embeddings and LSTM output, which often stabilizes training and improves generalization. I'll keep the classic num_skills output head to stay compatible with the proven DKT formulation. Decision: Write a clean LSTM-DKT model with LayerNorm, a complete train.py with early stopping and ReduceLROnPlateau, and a matching predict.py. Train for up to 50 epochs with patience=7, then submit eval.

Observation: Iteration 6 achieved a new best AUC-ROC of 0.7594 with the LSTM-DKT (hidden=256, dropout=0.3, LayerNorm). Training loss was still decreasing at epoch 20 (0.51), but early stopping triggered because val AUC plateaued around 0.762. Analysis: The simple additive embedding (skill_emb + correct_emb) may limit expressivity. Standard DKT uses a combined interaction embedding (skill*2+correct). Increasing capacity to hidden=512 and adding a small MLP head with stronger regularization (dropout=0.4, weight_decay=1e-3) could better fit the data while maintaining generalization. Decision: Rewrite model.py to use a combined interaction embedding table, larger LSTM (hidden=512), and an MLP head. Keep train.py and predict.py unchanged. Train for up to 80 epochs with patience=10 and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 7 achieved AUC=0.7486, which is worse than the best AUC=0.7594 from iteration 6. The MLP head and larger hidden_dim=512 from iter 7 seem to have hurt generalization. Analysis: We need to revert to a simpler architecture closer to iteration 6's best-performing setup. The classic DKT formulation with combined interaction embeddings, LSTM, LayerNorm, and a direct linear output head (no MLP) should perform better. We'll use hidden=256, embed=256, layers=2, dropout=0.3 with patience=10 and up to 80 epochs to allow more training before early stopping. Decision: Rewrite model.py to remove the MLP head, keep combined embeddings + LayerNorm, and run training with patience=10. Then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 8 produced AUC=0.7429, below the best 0.7594 from iteration 6. The single-model LSTM-DKT seems to plateau around 0.755-0.760 with significant variance across runs. Analysis: Given the small model size (~1M params) and fast training (~30s for 20 epochs), I can train an ensemble of 5 models with different random seeds and regularization tweaks, then average their predictions at inference time. Ensembling typically improves AUC by 1-3 points without architectural changes. I'll increase dropout to 0.4 and weight decay to 1e-3 to reduce overfitting, add embedding dropout, and train 5 models sequentially in one iteration. Decision: Update model.py (add embed dropout), train.py (support seed and custom save path), and predict.py (support directory-based ensemble loading and averaging). Run a loop to train 5 models with seeds 0-4 for up to 50 epochs each, then submit the ensemble directory for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 trained an ensemble of 5 models with heavy regularization (dropout=0.4, weight_decay=1e-3) and achieved AUC=0.7543, below the best single-model score of 0.7594 from iteration 6. The individual ensemble models had val AUCs around 0.747-0.749, indicating too much regularization caused underfitting. Analysis: The current architecture (2-layer LSTM, combined embeddings, LayerNorm) is solid, but dropout=0.4 + weight_decay=1e-3 suppresses performance. By reducing regularization (dropout=0.2, weight_decay=0) and training longer with patience=12, the model should fit better and surpass 0.7594. If this single run succeeds, I can scale to an ensemble in the next iteration. Decision: Run train.py with reduced regularization and longer training, then immediately submit eval on the resulting best_model.pt.

The best score remains 0.7594 from iteration 6, which used lighter regularization. The current model.py also has an `embed_dropout` layer added in iteration 9 that was not present in iteration 6. Analysis: We need to revert to the iteration 6 regime: combined interaction embeddings, LayerNorm, 2-layer LSTM (hidden=256, embed=256), dropout=0.3, NO embedding dropout, and minimal weight decay. This should recover ~0.759 AUC. After that, we can explore ensembles or slight capacity increases. Decision: Update model.py to remove `embed_dropout` and keep defaults at dropout=0.3. Update train.py defaults to dropout=0.3, weight_decay=0.0, patience=10. Run training for up to 60 epochs and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 11 achieved only 0.7432 AUC, well below the best 0.7594 from iteration 6. The LSTM-DKT architecture seems sound but recent runs consistently underperform. Analysis: A likely root cause is that the LSTM processes zero-padded timesteps as real interactions (skill 0, incorrect) because our embedding mapping never truly masks padding in the recurrence. By using pack_padded_sequence with the actual sequence lengths, the LSTM will ignore padded positions entirely, eliminating hidden-state contamination. This principled fix should recover (and potentially exceed) the 0.7594 peak. Decision: Update model.py to accept lengths and use pack_padded_sequence/ pad_packed_sequence with proper sorting/unsorting. Update train.py and predict.py to pass lengths, with backward compatibility for old checkpoints. Train with the proven config (embed=256, hidden=256, layers=2, dropout=0.3) for up to 60 epochs and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The best score so far is 0.7594 from iteration 6, achieved by a simple 2-layer LSTM-DKT without pack_padded_sequence. Recent iterations (7-12) that added complexity (MLP head, Transformer, pack_padded_sequence, heavy regularization) all underperformed, suggesting the original LSTM formulation is strongest. The iteration time is ~40-60s, leaving budget for ~10-15 more experiments. Analysis: Given the high variance across seeds and the clear plateau of single models around 0.755-0.760, the highest-probability path to beat 0.7594 is to (1) revert model.py to the simple LSTM-DKT architecture without pack_padded_sequence (matching the iteration 6 regime), and (2) train a small ensemble of 3 models with different seeds, then average predictions. Ensembling reduces variance and typically adds 1-3 AUC points. Decision: Rewrite model.py to a clean LSTM-DKT without lengths/packing. Keep train.py and predict.py compatible. Train 3 models (seeds 0, 1, 2) sequentially with moderate regularization (dropout=0.3, weight_decay=0) and save them to the ensemble/ directory. Then submit the ensemble for evaluation.

Analysis: Before any further training or eval submission, I must know exactly which checkpoints match the current code. The mismatch is likely from an earlier iteration that reserved an extra embedding index (e.g., padding_idx). Decision: Write and run a quick diagnostic script that attempts to load every checkpoint with the current model.py and prints the result. Then I'll clean up or fix predict.py accordingly.

trajectory.description: Observation: The iteration 15 diagnostic showed that best_model.pt and ensemble/model_3.pt/model_4.pt have an incompatible embedding shape ([299,256] vs current code's [298,256]), while ensemble/model_0-2.pt load correctly. We never obtained an eval for the 3-model ensemble trained in iteration 14 because the bad checkpoints caused a RuntimeError during load. Analysis: By removing the incompatible files and making predict.py robust (skip unloadable checkpoints with a warning), we can safely evaluate the existing three compatible models. These were trained with the clean LSTM-DKT, dropout=0.3, and seeds 0-2, so their averaged predictions should reduce variance and likely exceed the single-model best of 0.7594. Decision: (1) overwrite predict.py with robust loading, (2) delete incompatible checkpoints, (3) run a quick smoke test, and (4) submit eval on the cleaned ensemble/ directory.

Observation: Iteration 16 evaluated the 3-model ensemble at 0.7517 AUC, still below the best single-model score of 0.7594 from iteration 6. The diagnostic confirmed that ensemble/model_0-2.pt load correctly, but their individual performance was likely around 0.745-0.750. Analysis: The current LSTM-DKT architecture (clean, with LayerNorm, no MLP head, no packing) is sound but recent runs with patience=10 or bad seeds underperformed. Iteration 6 used patience=7 and got 0.7594. The key insight is that early stopping with moderate patience (5-7) prevents overfitting. With 17 minutes and 14 iterations left, I should run a small hyperparameter/seed sweep with the proven architecture, keeping patience tight. I'll train 4 models sequentially with different seeds and configs, track validation AUC, and keep the best single model for submission. This maximizes the chance of finding a seed/config that exceeds 0.7594.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 17 trained a 4-model seed sweep. The best validation AUC was only 0.7464, and no eval was submitted for these models. The overall best eval AUC remains 0.7594 from iteration 6. With ~15 minutes and 13 iterations left, I need a meaningful architectural change to break the plateau. Analysis: Vanilla LSTM-DKT has plateaued around 0.755-0.760 across many seeds and regularization settings. Adding a causal self-attention layer over the LSTM outputs lets the model explicitly attend to relevant past interactions when predicting the next response, which should improve long-range dependency modeling without adding excessive parameters. Decision: Rewrite model.py to augment the 2-layer LSTM with causal scaled dot-product self-attention (residual connection + LayerNorm). Keep train.py and predict.py unchanged for compatibility. Train a single model with the proven hyperparameters (hidden=256, embed=256, dropout=0.3) but longer training (100 epochs, patience=15) to let the new capacity converge, then submit eval immediately.

Recent single-model experiments consistently plateau around 0.743-0.750 except for the isolated iteration 6 peak. Analysis: The core LSTM-DKT architecture with LayerNorm (no attention, no MLP, no packing) is still the most promising. A likely overlooked issue is that padded time-steps (skill=0, correct=0) pass through the embedding lookup just like real interactions, feeding misleading gradients into the LSTM. By zeroing-out embeddings at padded positions using sequence lengths, the LSTM receives cleaner inputs. This is a lightweight, principled fix that avoids the complexity/bugs of pack_padded_sequence. To exploit random-seed variance within our remaining budget (~14 min, 12 iters), I'll run three independent training runs in one iteration and keep the best validation model. Decision: Revert model.py to the simple 2-layer LSTM-DKT with LayerNorm, add optional embedding masking via lengths. Update train.py and predict.py to pass lengths. Train three models (seeds 0,1,2) sequentially with patience=7, pick the best via validation AUC, copy to best_model.pt, and submit eval.

trajectory.command:
python train.py --seed=0 --save_path=m0.pt && python train.py --seed=1 --save_path=m1.pt && python train.py --seed=2 --save_path=m2.pt && python -c "
import torch, shutil
best_auc = -1.0
best_path = None
for p in ['m0.pt','m1.pt','m2.pt']:
    ckpt = torch.load(p, map_location='cpu')
    auc = ckpt.get('val_auc', -1)
    print(f'{p}: val_auc={auc:.4f}')
    if auc > best_auc:
        best_auc = auc
        best_path = p
print(f'Best: {best_path} ({best_auc:.4f})')
shutil.copy(best_path, 'best_model.pt')
"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 19 scored 0.7424 AUC, well below the best 0.7594 from iteration 6. Across iterations 7-19, every architectural tweak (combined embeddings, MLP head, attention, packing, embedding masking) has underperformed the iteration 6 peak. Analysis: Iteration 7's reasoning explicitly notes that iteration 6 used additive embeddings (skill_emb + correct_emb), while all subsequent iterations switched to combined interaction embeddings (skill*2+correct). The switch away from additive embeddings coincides exactly with the performance drop and never recovered. To match the best-performing regime, I must revert model.py to additive embeddings and remove the embedding masking/packing complexity. Decision: Rewrite model.py with additive skill+correctness embeddings, LayerNorm, and a simple 2-layer LSTM (hidden=256, embed=256, dropout=0.3), ignoring the lengths parameter for full compatibility with existing train.py/predict.py. Train 3 models with seeds 0-2, patience=7, keep the best validation model, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 24 achieved a new best test AUC of 0.7658 with a 15-model ensemble (ensemble_15). Remaining time is only ~2.3 minutes with 6 iterations left—too tight for training new models from scratch (3 models take ~2 min). Analysis: Rather than risking a timeout by training more models for diminishing returns, I can improve the existing ensemble by optimizing combination weights on the validation set. Current predict.py uses val_auc as a proxy for weighting, but weights that minimize validation BCE (or maximize validation AUC directly) often produce better test performance. Because the models are already trained, this costs only inference time (~10-20 s) and is very safe timewise. Decision: Write optimize_bce.py to load every model in ensemble_15/, compute validation predictions, find weights that minimize BCE (and compare with uniform and val_auc weighting), save the best weights.json into ensemble_15/, and update predict.py to read weights.json when present. Then submit eval on ensemble_15/.

trajectory.description: Observation: The training command in iteration 26 failed immediately because train.py accepts --epochs, not --max_epochs. We now have only ~84 seconds (0.0234 h) and 4 iterations left. Training even a single model takes ~40–50 s, leaving almost no margin for evaluation, so a new training run risks being killed by the time budget. Analysis: Our strongest artifact is the ensemble_15 directory, which produced the best test AUC so far of 0.7658. All 15 models (m0..m14.pt) inside it are compatible with the current predict.py and model.py. There is no faster way to improve than to re-submit this proven ensemble. Decision: Skip any training or file changes and immediately submit ensemble_15 for evaluation to lock in the best score without risking a timeout.
